title: The Macro Minute: Should the risk-on market regime continue?

Happy Thursday out there, team 42. It's
your skipper here, Darius Dale, to
present our macro minute for Thursday,
April 30th, 2026. As always, we'll start
with the executive summary from today's
lead-off morning note. So, let's dive
right in. Today's key macro question is,
did the most impactful 24-hour period of
macro and micro data in history support
a continuation of the risk-on market
regime?
Our answer is yes for now. The historic
spate of US macro data and corporate
results generally affirmed our bullish
resilient US economy, paradigm C,
productivity boom, and jobless recovery
themes. Big tech's AI arms race is
catalyzing an unprecedented CapEx
supercycle with aggregate spending
guidance from Amazon, Alphabet, Meta,
and Microsoft surging to $715 billion in
2026, nearly double the $376 billion
they deployed last year.
Elsewhere, the ECB and Bank of England
out-hawked the marginally hawkish Fed,
resulting in a sharp decline in the US
dollar, which is positive for global
liquidity. Ideally, the US dollar
declines because of accelerating global
growth and outward-bound capital flows,
but the probability of this more
sustainable outcome occurring over the
medium term is low and falling. As such,
investors must be prepared to reduce
risk in the coming weeks if today's
accelerated US-Iran brinksmanship
devolves into a resumption of kinetic
conflict, which would likely prolong the
closure of the Strait of Hormuz and
transfer the elevated sudden stop risk
embedded supply chains to the global
liquidity cycle.
As always, a wrap-up I'll end with a
question from our community. This one's
titled, uh, "Trim PCE, ignore Hormuz?"
Says, "Working through Warsh's framework
and stuck on one piece. Dallas Fed
trimming PCE is at 2.3%, headline CPI is
at 3.3% and rising with Brent at $115 a
barrel and Hormuz effectively closed,
double closed.
Trim PCE equals ignore Hormuz? That is
the framework working as designed,
Warsh's framework." The question I
cannot resolve is if Warsh cuts into
re-accelerating headline CPI on a 2.3%
trim mean rate, does the long end accept
the productivity story or does this the
moment yields blow out and force your
curve control? Uh, so, outstanding
question, love this question. A couple
things I'll say to start, which is
number one, Warsh is not going to come
in and cut anything as we saw with
yesterday's uh, descent uh, uh, from the
Fed, uh, very clear that the room is
sort of splintering apart, which is
something we called for going back to
uh, uh, last fall in terms of the the
room, the the the the policy rate
getting a lot closer to a lot of the the
high end of the estimates of neutral.
So, you already have a lot of Fed
officials who don't want to continue
easing to begin with. And oh, by the
way, now we're in an energy supply shock
that should uh, provide some upward
drift on headline inflation that may in
subsequent months spill into underlying
measures of inflation like trim mean
PCE, median CPI, uh, etc. Right now,
they're being uh, the the the the
energy price uh, spike is not uh, being
adequately picked up by these sort of
underlying measures of inflation, but
history shows is that, you know,
typically what you did you you do see
some uh, pass-through and that
pass-through is likely to be
accelerated, of course, if the Fed uh,
opts to uh, ease uh, policy uh, here,
but in our opinion, that's a low
probability event uh, based on a variety
of factors, uh, not not most notably uh,
market pricing as well as the forward
guidance that we received uh, yesterday.
Uh, and then the final thing I'll say on
this is um, with regards to the long end
accepting the productivity story, uh,
the jury's still out on whether the long
end of the Treasury curve and FOMC in
general buys into the uh, Kevin Warsh
argument that higher productivity equals
lower inflation and lower rates across
the curve. Uh, that is one view. I
generally have a sympathy with that view
based on a variety of our other
fundamental research themes, but it's
not a clear-cut uh, um,
you know, straightforward answer. Uh,
so, I think the markets and and and the
Fed itself are going to have to, you
know, roll up their sleeves and dig into
the data in the coming quarters and
years to really ascertain what the most
likely probability uh, of that outcome
is. In our view, we think there is scope
to cut rates and there is scope for uh,
the bond bond yields to uh, drift lower
alongside that, but again, that's not
the only factor impacting bond yields.
The most important factor, as we all
know here, is the geopolitically driven
supply-demand imbalance in the Treasury
bond market that remains ongoing and
will likely accelerate uh, in the coming
quarters and years. So, we're wrapping
up there. Darius Dale here presenting
our macro minute for Thursday, April
30th, 2026.
We'll be publishing our uh, May 2026
macro scouting report presentation to
our community, our global investor
community this afternoon. So, uh, stay
tuned for that. It's going to be a
fantastic presentation. I'm looking
forward to it. Cheers.
